title: AI Agent vs. Subscription Hell: A Cross-Platform SaaS Audit Battle Report
summary: A $200/yr Substack bundle renewal triggered a subscription audit across Gmail, Outlook, Substack, and Gamma. The AI agent hit a brick wall with Google Passkey, discovered Gmail MCP is 10x faster than Outlook Playwright, and ultimately distilled hard-won lessons back into reusable cli-x skills.
body:
# AI Agent vs. Subscription Hell: A Cross-Platform SaaS Audit Battle Report

"Last year I subscribed to a bundle on Lenny's Newsletter, and now I'm finding out each one needs to be unsubscribed individually or I'll get charged. This is killing me."

That sentence kicked off a 26-hour marathon across 4 context compactions, spanning Gmail / Outlook / Substack / Gamma in a full SaaS subscription audit. I put Claude Code to work as my agent, armed with Playwright MCP and Gmail MCP to hunt down every paid subscription and cancel them one by one.

The results? Some parts went surprisingly smooth. Others hit brick walls.

## Ground Zero: The $200 Lenny's Newsletter Problem

The situation was straightforward. Two email accounts — xingjingx@gmail.com and surixing@outlook.com — scattered with various SaaS subscriptions. Lenny's Newsletter was the biggest ticket item at $200/yr, tied to surixing@outlook.com, about to auto-renew.

My instructions to the agent were equally direct: "Can you go through my accounts one by one and unsubscribe? Just check with me before each one."

## First Discovery: Gmail MCP >> Playwright

Step one of the subscription audit was finding all payment records. Two email accounts, two different approaches:

**Gmail (xingjingx@gmail.com)**: Using Gmail MCP's `search_threads` tool, a simple `from:stripe.com receipt` query directly returned all Stripe receipts. 3 seconds, crystal clear results:

- Gamma — $96/yr Plus plan
- Manus AI — $19/mo (stopped after Oct 2025)
- Lenny's Newsletter — $200/yr
- Black Forest Labs, flux-ai.io and other small subscriptions

**Outlook (surixing@outlook.com)**: No Graph API token meant falling back to Playwright automation on Outlook Web. First tried `invoice OR receipt OR subscription renewal OR payment confirmed` — returned nothing. Outlook Web's search engine doesn't support OR syntax at all. Finally used the most basic `receipt from` and got results — just Lenny's Newsletter.

The contrast was stark: **Gmail MCP is structured API queries, Outlook Web is DOM manipulation + unreliable search boxes.** Same task, 3 seconds vs 10 minutes of fumbling around.

> **Universal Stripe receipt pattern**: Every SaaS product using Stripe payments sends receipts from `invoice+statements+acct_xxx@stripe.com`. This means `from:stripe.com receipt` is a universal audit query — regardless of what products you're subscribed to, if they use Stripe for billing, this one search catches them all.

## Canceling Substack: The Magic Link Shortcut

After finding Lenny's Newsletter in the Stripe receipts, next step was cancellation.

The standard Substack flow would be going to `substack.com/settings/payments`. But that requires login first — Substack uses magic link authentication, meaning the agent would need to enter email, wait for the magic link email, then go to the inbox and click the link.

But we discovered a shortcut: **Stripe receipt emails have "Manage your subscription" links at the bottom that contain magic tokens. Clicking these takes you directly to the subscription management page, no additional login required.**

Agent used Gmail MCP to find receipt → extract management link → Playwright opens link → directly into Substack's subscription management page → click cancel → confirm. The whole flow was seamless.

## Google Passkey: Automation's Brick Wall

Gamma's $96/yr Plus plan wasn't so lucky.

Gamma uses Google OAuth for login. Agent opened gamma.app/settings, got redirected to Google login, entered xingjingx@gmail.com... then Google demanded passkey verification — fingerprint or face recognition.

This is a hard limit Playwright cannot bypass. There was no "Try another way" to fall back to password input (depends on user security settings). Passkey is a hardware-bound challenge that browser automation tools are completely powerless against.

The agent dutifully reported: "Google Passkey verification cannot be automated, you'll need to manually go to gamma.app/settings → Billing to cancel."

This discovery has implications beyond just Gamma — **any platform using Google OAuth where users have enabled passkey will hit this wall.** It's a fundamental limitation of agent automation.

## Human Course Correction: Agents Aren't Infallible

Throughout the process, I had to correct the agent at least three times.

First time: agent searched Gmail for Lenny's Newsletter subscription records, couldn't find any. I said "I definitely bought Lenny's, look more carefully". Turns out that subscription was tied to surixing@outlook.com, not in Gmail.

Second time: I asked the agent to check surixing@outlook.com for other subscriptions, agent got confused and executed operations on Gmail instead. I said directly: "I meant the second thing, you got the wrong email, since you have the new one, go search it yourself, or just look through emails one by one if needed."

Third time: agent suggested I manually check various product websites for Product Pass tool subscriptions (Lovable, Cursor etc.). My response: "Why don't YOU do it??? Why would I???????"

These corrections were very real. **Agents in cross-platform, cross-account scenarios easily lose track of context.** Especially after session compaction (this session went through 4 compactions), the agent's memory of "which email goes with which task" degrades. Human oversight here isn't optional — it's essential.

## Crystallization: From One-off Operation to Reusable Skills

After the audit finished, I had the agent check if this was building toward something called a cli-x skill.

cli-x is a "universal external platform interface" — a skill that unifies all external platform operations into `/cli-x <platform> <operation>` commands. It already has 18 platform runbooks covering everything from GitHub (CLI approach) to WeChat Official Accounts (Playwright approach).

The hard-won experience from this session directly patched two runbooks:

**substack.md**: The unsubscribe operation changed from "go to settings/payments" to "prioritize using Gmail MCP to find Stripe receipt → click manage subscription magic link" pathway. Known Issues added Gmail MCP audit patterns and Google Passkey blocking.

**outlook.md**: Added "Playwright Fallback Notes" section — Outlook Web doesn't support OR queries, search results have tab categories, Focused vs Other inbox gotchas.

## Cost and Efficiency

Session statistics:
- Duration: ~26 hours (across two days, with significant idle time)
- Token consumption: 73M total (including 69.5M cache reads)
- LLM calls: 681
- Tool calls: 440 (Playwright operations dominated: snapshot 90 times, navigate 60 times, click 34 times)
- Estimated cost: ~$56

$56 to audit all SaaS subscriptions across two email accounts, cancel Lenny's Newsletter ($200/yr), discover Gamma ($96/yr needs manual cancellation), and confirm the Outlook account had no other paid subscriptions. From a purely economic angle, the first year of avoided renewals already paid for itself.

But the bigger value is what crystallized: **two cli-x skill runbooks now have battle-tested operational pathways**. Next time anyone does similar work, they won't need to rediscover these pitfalls.

## Takeaways

1. **`from:stripe.com receipt` is the universal SaaS subscription audit query.** As long as products use Stripe for billing (the vast majority of SaaS do), this one search catches everything.

2. **MCP > CLI > Playwright priority order is real.** What Gmail MCP accomplished in 3 seconds took Outlook Playwright 10 minutes of fumbling. Use APIs when possible, avoid DOM manipulation.

3. **Google Passkey is a brick wall for browser automation.** Not "hard to bypass" — "impossible to bypass." When designing agent workflows, passkey must be listed as a known limitation with manual fallback for users.

4. **Agents need human oversight in cross-platform, cross-account scenarios.** Context compaction degrades agent memory of "which account does what task." Trust but verify.

5. **One-off operations + experience crystallization = compound returns.** Do the work, then write it into runbooks. Next time you (or any agent) do the same thing, no need to rediscover Stripe receipt patterns or magic link shortcuts.
